title: Anti-Competitive Agreements
content:
Session 2: Anti Competitive Agreements 
 
Steel Authority [2010 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč An informant alleged that SAIL had entered into an exclusive supply agreement with the Indian Railways for rails. This was 
said to constitute: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Abuse of dominant position (Section 4), and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Foreclosure of competition for other suppliers. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs Initial Steps Under Section 26 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI registered the information and asked the informant to file an affidavit ‚Äď delay in reply by opponents SO 
ordered DG investigation under 26(1) + allowed SAIL to file its reply before the DG. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SAIL Seeks Interim Hearing Before CCI 
‚Ė†‚Äč
SAIL filed an interim reply + an application seeking a hearing before any interim order could be passed. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CCI refused to hear SAIL at this stage. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SAIL Appeals to COMPAT (now NCLAT) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI applied for impleadment, arguing: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
It must be a party to the appeal, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The appeal is not maintainable because 26(1) directions to DG are NOT appealable under s.53A. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč COMPAT‚Äôs Decision ‚Üí CCI is not a necessary or proper party + appeal is maintainable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI Appeals to Supreme Court. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether orders under Section 26(1) (directing DG investigation) are appealable under Section 53A. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether forming a prima facie opinion under Section 26(1) requires notice/hearing (natural justice). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the CCI is a necessary or proper party in appeals before the Tribunal. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč What is the scope and threshold for the CCI‚Äôs interim order power under Section 33. 
86 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the CCI must record reasons while forming a prima facie opinion. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether investigations/inquiries must be conducted within strict time limits. 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Competition Act, 2002 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 26(1) ‚ÄĒ Prima facie opinion; direction to DG for investigation 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 26(2) & (6) ‚ÄĒ Orders closing cases or finding contravention (appealable) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 33 ‚ÄĒ Power to issue interim orders 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 53A ‚ÄĒ Appeal only against specific final/ intermediate orders [s 26(2) / (6). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 57 ‚ÄĒ Confidentiality obligations 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI (General) Regulations (pre-2024) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reg. 16 ‚ÄĒ 15-day limit for first meeting; 60 days for prima facie decision 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reg. 17 ‚ÄĒ Preliminary conference 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reg. 18(2) ‚ÄĒ Defines when an "inquiry" begins 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reg. 20 ‚ÄĒ DG must file report ordinarily within 60 days 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reg. 31(2) ‚ÄĒ Interim orders must have short returnable dates 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reg. 35 ‚ÄĒ Confidentiality 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 1 ‚ÄĒ Appealability of 26(1) Orders 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 53A lists only specific orders as appealable ‚ÄĒ 26(2) and 26(6) are included ‚Üí Section 26(1) is not included 
‚Üí Legislature intentionally excluded. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A direction to investigate does not decide legal rights, nor is it adjudicatory. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CCI proceedings are in rem, not inter partes, so early-stage orders don‚Äôt attract appeal. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč HERE ‚Üí the order is administrative, procedural, and non-final ‚Üí Not appealable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 2 ‚ÄĒ Natural Justice at 26(1) Stage 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 26(1) only requires the CCI to form a prima facie view, not an adjudicatory decision ‚Üí hence no hearing is 
required, and parties cannot insist on it. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
BUT the CCI may, at its discretion, seek assistance/information via: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Reg. 17(2) preliminary conference 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Calling other persons including affected parties 
‚ěĘ‚Äč HENCE ‚Üí no mandatory hearing; natural justice does not apply at 26(1). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 3 ‚ÄĒ Whether CCI is a Necessary or Proper Party 
‚ěĘ‚Äč When CCI initiates an inquiry suo motu ‚Üí necessary party. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč When proceedings arise from private information ‚Üí proper party, as its presence helps adjudication. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI is an expert body; its input aids the Tribunal ‚Üí CCI should be joined either as a necessary or proper party 
depending on the case. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 4 ‚ÄĒ Scope of Interim Orders under Section 33 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Higher threshold than 26(1): 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Interim orders can only be issued after a 26(1) direction is passed. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CCI must record: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Higher degree of satisfaction than prima facie 
‚Ė†‚Äč
That the act is ongoing/about to occur 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Necessity of interim restraint 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Likelihood of irreparable harm or adverse effect on competition 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Must issue fast returnable dates (Reg. 31(2)). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč WHY?? Interim orders can drastically change the market ‚ÄĒ especially in tech or entertainment sectors ‚Üí must be 
used sparingly. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 5 ‚ÄĒ Necessity to Record Reasons 
87 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC‚Äôs principle of administrative law: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Even prima facie orders under 26(1) must record some reasons. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Final/interim orders must be speaking orders responding to party submissions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rationale: ensures transparency, accountability, and judicial review. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 6 ‚ÄĒ Speedy Investigation 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Prima facie opinion to be formed much before the 60-day outer limit. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The entire investigation/inquiry must be completed as expeditiously as possible. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Final order after interim order must be issued within 60 days. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč DG‚Äôs report must be submitted within 45 days (or as directed). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Maintain strict confidentiality under s.57 and Reg. 35. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 26(1) orders are NOT appealable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No right to a hearing exists at the stage of forming a prima facie view. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI is a necessary/proper party in most appeals. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Interim orders under Section 33 require a very high threshold, must be reasoned, and used rarely. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI must record reasons even for prima facie orders. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Investigations must be speedy; DG‚Äôs report ordinarily within 45‚Äď60 days. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Confidentiality obligations are strict and enforceable. 
 
CCI v Kerala Film Exhibitors [2025 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč An information was filed before the Competition Commission of India (CCI) by Crown Theatre (Respondent No. 4). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Allegations were made against: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Kerala Film Exhibitors Federation (KFEF), and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Its office-bearers, namely the President (Respondent No. 2) and General Secretary (Respondent No. 3). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč It was alleged that: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Film distributors were threatened that their films would not be screened in cinema halls owned by KFEF 
members if they supplied films to the Crown Theatre ‚Üí amounted to a concerted boycott aimed at 
excluding Crown Theatre from the market. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Pursuant to an order of the CCI under Section 26, the DG conducted an investigation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DG‚Äôs findings: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč KFEF had contravened Section 3(3) of the Competition Act, 2002. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Respondents 2 and 3 played an active role in facilitating and enforcing the anti-competitive conduct. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs Order (08-09-2015): 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Held that KFEF violated Section 3(1) read with Section 3(3)(b) (limiting or controlling supply / market access). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Held Respondents 2 and 3 liable under Section 48 as persons in charge of and responsible for KFEF. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Imposed: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Monetary penalty, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Behavioural remedy directing: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
KFEF not to associate Respondents 2 and 3 with its affairs for 2 years, and 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Respondents 2 and 3 not to associate with KFEF. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal before COMPAT filed jointly by Respondents 1‚Äď3. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč COMPAT upheld CCI‚Äôs findings on merits and penalty on KFEF. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč However, COMPAT set aside penalties and behavioural directions against Respondents 2 and 3 on the ground of 
violation of natural justice, holding that: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No separate opportunity of hearing / second show cause notice on penalty was given to them. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Statutory appeal under Section 53T: 
88 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Filed before the SC by the CCI, challenging the COMPAT order insofar as it diluted penalties and behavioural 
remedies against Respondents 2 and 3. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the notice dated 10-06-2015 issued by the CCI constituted a valid and sufficient notice to Respondents 2 and 3 for 
the purpose of imposing penalties under Section 27 read with Section 48 of the Competition Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether principles of natural justice require a second show cause notice specifically proposing the penalty before imposing: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Monetary penalties, and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Behavioural remedies under Section 27. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the CCI has the statutory power to impose behavioural and structural remedies, including directions affecting 
individuals, under Section 27 of the Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the behavioural directions imposed on Respondents 2 and 3 were disproportionate or illegal. 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Statutory Provisions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(1) & 3(3)(b) ‚ÄĒ Prohibits anti-competitive agreements, including agreements limiting supply or market 
access. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 19 ‚ÄĒ Inquiry into anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominance. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 26 ‚ÄĒ Procedure for inquiry; DG investigation and issuance of notice. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 27 ‚ÄĒ Powers of CCI post-inquiry, including: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Cease-and-desist orders, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Monetary penalties, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Behavioral and structural remedies. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 48 ‚ÄĒ Liability of persons in charge of and responsible for the conduct of a company‚Äôs affairs. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 53A / 53T ‚ÄĒ Appellate framework (COMPAT ‚Üí Supreme Court). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulatory Framework 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Competition Commission of India (General) Regulations, 2009: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulation 21 ‚ÄĒ Issuance of notice post DG report. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulation 22 ‚ÄĒ Consideration of objections. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulation 48 ‚ÄĒ Proceedings against individuals under Section 48. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Competition Commission of India (General) Regulations, 2024: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulation 22 amended. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulation 48 renumbered as Regulation 49 (post-2024). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Doctrinal Principles 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Audi alteram partem (opportunity of hearing). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Proportionality in regulatory penalties. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Distinction between: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Primary penalty on enterprise, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Incidental / corollary consequences on individuals. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Validity of the Notice dated 10-06-2015 ‚Üí examined whether Respondents 2 and 3 were given adequate notice and 
opportunity. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The notice was issued under Section 26 read with Regulation 21. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Clearly referred to contraventions recorded in the DG Report. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Explicitly identified Respondents 2 and 3 as persons in charge of KFEF‚Äôs affairs. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Invited replies and objections. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since: 
89 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The CCI concurred with the DG‚Äôs findings, a detailed disagreement notice (as required in cases of 
divergence) was not necessary ‚Üí analogised this to service jurisprudence, where a disciplinary authority 
must issue a disagreement note only when it differs from the inquiry officer. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SO ‚Üí the notice dated 10-06-2015 was legally sufficient. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Requirement of a Second Show Cause Notice on Penalty 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The CA does not mandate a second show cause notice proposing penalty  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 27 empowers the CCI to impose penalties after inquiry. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Introducing an additional penalty-specific hearing would: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Undermine the efficacy and deterrence of the Act, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Enable dilatory tactics, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Risk regulated entities presenting a fait accompli. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court emphasised that time is of the essence in competition enforcement. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulatory effectiveness cannot be sacrificed at the altar of procedural formalism. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SO ‚Üí no violation of natural justice occurred. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Power to Impose Behavioural and Structural Remedies 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court undertook a purposive interpretation of Section 27. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 27‚Äôs opening clause, read with clauses (a), (b), (d), (e), and (g), confers wide remedial powers ‚Üí 
objective is to effectively end the contravention, not merely punish past conduct. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Behavioural remedies are primarily imposed on the enterprise, however: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
When such remedies impact corporate governance, corollary directions against individuals become 
necessary. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Without such power, behavioral remedies would be unenforceable. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, directions preventing Respondents 2 and 3 from associating with KFEF were incidental and consequential, 
not independent penalties ‚áí CCI has full authority to impose such remedies. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Proportionality of Penalty 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court assessed: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Monetary penalty (10% of average income) ‚Üí was meagre 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Behavioural remedy of disassociation. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Conduct had serious anti-competitive effects: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Market foreclosure, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Harm to informant, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Prejudice to consumers. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Respondents continued anti-competitive conduct, showing lack of deterrence. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court held: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Mere monetary penalty was insufficient. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Behavioral remedy was necessary to protect competition and consumers. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SO ‚Üí Penalty was proportionate and justified. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Supreme Court: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Allowed the appeal filed by the CCI. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Set aside the COMPAT judgment insofar as it diluted penalties and behavioural remedies against Respondents 2 
and 3. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Restored the CCI‚Äôs order in its entirety. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Directions: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč KFEF shall not associate Respondents 2 and 3 with its administration, management or governance. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Respondents 2 and 3 shall not associate with KFEF. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The two-year disassociation period shall run from 01-12-2025. 
 
90 
 
Excel Crop Care Supreme Court of India [2017 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FCI wrote to the CCI complaining of suspected cartelisation among four manufacturers of Aluminium Phosphide Tablets 
(APT) ‚Äď used for grain fumigation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Allegation was that for 8+ years, these firms quoted identical prices in tenders; alleged bid rigging and collusive bidding 
under s.3(3)(d). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Market structure 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Only four suppliers of APT in India ‚Üí low number of actors + repeated identical pricing ‚Üí red flag. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč DG analysed tenders from 2002 to 2009: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Identical pricing pattern (except 2007) ‚Üí this pattern was near-perfect synchronisation, despite different 
cost bases. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Suspicious behaviour during bid submission (2009) ‚Üí representatives of all three appellants made 
common entries in the FCI visitor register. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
One employee, S.K. Bose of Excel Crop Care, signed on behalf of representatives of other competitors as 
well. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč ‚ÄúCollective boycott‚ÄĚ in 2011 tender 
‚ěĘ‚Äč All manufacturers refused to bid claiming ‚Äúonerous tender terms.‚ÄĚ ‚Äď BUT they had earlier been supplied under 
even more onerous conditions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Notice of non-participation came one day before the deadline. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Collective boycott = form of bid rigging (concerted refusal). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI found contravention of s.3(3)(d)‚ÄĒbid rigging ‚áí penalty under s.27(b) (at 9% of average turnover for 3 years) 
[penalty was on total turnover ‚Üí severe for multi-product companies] 
‚ěĘ‚Äč COMPAT affirmed CCI on merits, BUT modified penalty ‚Üí must be on relevant turnover, not total/global 
turnover. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Both sides appealed. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether there was an anti-competitive ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ under s.3(3)(d) (bid rigging / collusive bidding) in the tender process? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether penalty under s.27(b) must be based on: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Total/global turnover of multi-product companies, or 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Relevant turnover (turnover relating only to the contravening product)? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Statutory Provisions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.3(1): Prohibits agreements causing AAEC. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.3(3)(d): Presumption of AAEC for bid rigging/collusive bidding. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Explanation to s.3(3): Defines ‚Äúbid rigging.‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.27(b): CCI can impose a penalty up to 10% of turnover of each enterprise for each year of continuance. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Doctrines Applied 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Noscitur a sociis ‚Üí interpret ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ broadly, considering surrounding words. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Parallelism Plus Test (Dyestuffs Case, ECJ): 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Mere parallel behaviour ‚Č† cartel. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
But parallelism + evidentiary plus factors = concerted practice. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Purposive interpretation of competition statutes. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Doctrine of proportionality (Arts 14 & 21). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Deterrence vs. death of enterprise principle. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Existence of an Anti-Competitive Agreement 
91 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Parallelism doctrine applies to market pricing, not to bid tenders. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Bids require independent, confidential pricing ‚Üí identical bids across 10 years = ‚Äútoo much coincidence.‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Factors indicating collusion ‚Äď the Court highlighted 6 powerful plus-factors: 
Factor 
Significance 
10-year history of identical pricing 
Not explainable by market forces. 
Only 4 producers in market 
Facilitates cartelisation. 
Different costs of production, yet identical bids 
Impossible 
under 
independent 
commercial 
behaviour. 
Different geographical locations, yet same price 
Eliminates cost-based explanations. 
Different profit margins, yet same price 
Shows central coordination. 
Different rates to different clients, but identical rates for 
FCI tenders 
Indicates bid-specific collusion. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
These satisfy the parallelism-plus standard. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Visitor Register Signature Evidence 
‚Ė†‚Äč
One representative signing for competitors ‚Č† innocent. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Direct administrative coordination ‚Üí powerful indicator of bid rigging. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč 2011 Collective Boycott = concerted practice 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The reason for the ‚Äúonerous terms‚ÄĚ was false. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Industry had complied with tougher terms earlier. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Timing (one day before) showed joint strategy. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Collective refusal to bid itself is a form of bid rigging. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Conclusion: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
There was an anti-competitive ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ under s.3(3)(d). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Presumption of AAEC holds; appellants failed to rebut. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Penalty ‚ÄĒ Relevant Turnover vs. Total/Global Turnover 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Statutory ambiguity 
‚Ė†‚Äč
s.27(b) uses the term ‚Äúturnover‚ÄĚ without specifying: turnover of what? Entire company? Product 
involved? Global turnover? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Held:  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Equity & proportionality 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Penalty must be proportionate to the wrong. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Not designed to kill the enterprise ‚Üí ‚Äúcivil death‚ÄĚ 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Penal provision‚Äôs purpose = deterrence, not destruction. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Nature of s.3 offences 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Cartels may relate to only one product, especially in multi-product companies. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Penalising all products = penalising innocent product lines. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Proportionality is constitutionally embedded 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Articles 14 and 21 demand proportionality. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Penalties cannot produce ‚Äúshocking results.‚ÄĚ 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Purposive interpretation 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Must link harm caused with profits derived. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Legislative intent: target anti-competitive conduct specifically, not enterprise globally. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Comparative reference 
92 
 
‚óŹ‚Äč
ASEAN guidelines on competition policy ‚Üí emphasise efficiency & proportionality (note: RS 
said India is not a member; Court used them illustratively). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Conclusion on Penalty 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Relevant turnover = turnover from products that formed the subject matter of cartel (APT). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Penalty properly = % of relevant turnover. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On Merits 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Supreme Court confirmed violation of s.3(3)(d) ‚Üí bid rigging proved. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs finding was upheld. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On Penalty 
‚ěĘ‚Äč COMPAT‚Äôs interpretation ‚ÄĒ use relevant turnover ‚ÄĒ upheld. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs use of total/global turnover was rejected as disproportionate. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Thus: Relevant turnover ‚Üí base; penalty capped at 10% thereof. 
 
Significance and Aftermath 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Excel Crop Care Doctrine (Original Rule) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Penalty must be based on: relevant turnover 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Then apply percentage considering: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Nature of cartel 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Duration 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Cooperation 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Mitigating/aggravating factors 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Legal maximum = 10% of turnover (interpreted as relevant turnover). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Overruling by Legislature  
‚ěĘ‚Äč 2023 Amendment ‚Üí Explanation II to s.27(b) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Penalty now based on ‚Äúglobal turnover from all products and services of the enterprise.‚ÄĚ 
‚óŹ‚Äč
This explicitly reverses Excel Crop Care for the future. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč So: Excel Crop Care still governs penalty for pre-2023 cases. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč But not for post-2023 contraventions. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 2022 CCI Monetary Penalty Guidelines 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court inspired CCI to formalise. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Two-Step Penalty Process 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Base Penalty 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Base = up to 30% of relevant turnover (as defined in guidelines: direct or indirect turnover 
relating to contravention). 
‚óŹ‚Äč
This is standardized. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Adjustment ‚Äď increase/decrease based on: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Duration 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Nature of breach 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Mitigation (cooperation, settlement) 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Aggravation (ringleader, repeated offence) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
But the final penalty cannot exceed the statutory cap, which is now 10% of global turnover 
(post-amendment). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Relevance ‚Äď even though Excel Crop Care used relevant turnover, the 2023 amendment allows global turnover as legal 
maximum. 
 
Himmatlal v CCI [2018 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
93 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In 2014, Western Coalfields released two tenders for coal/sand transportation ‚Üí Himmatlal submitted bids in both tenders 
but was not the lowest bidder (L1) ‚ÄĒ they lost on merit. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In June 2015, CCI issued a show cause notice under Section 19(1)(a) r/w Section 3 of the Competition Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Allegation ‚Üí Himmatlal colluded with nine other firms, engaging in cartelisation, bid-rigging, and anti-competitive 
agreements. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DG Investigation ‚Üí after considering Himmatlal‚Äôs reply, the CCI: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Formed a prima facie opinion under Section 26(1). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Directed the DG to investigate. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
DG conducted the inquiry and issued a report confirming cartel behaviour, holding Himmatlal guilty. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI Final Order 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI accepted the DG‚Äôs findings and imposed: ‚āĻ3.61 crore penalty on Himmatlal + Penalties on nine other firms. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal before NCLAT under Section 53B + an interim stay application 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT granted: Admission of appeal + Interim stay, conditional upon depositing 10% of penalty. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
BUT Himmatlal did not deposit 10% ‚ÄĒ argued severe financial hardship. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT: Gave extended time + warned that appeal may be dismissed. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Appellant filed an application for modification, pleading inability to pay. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT dismissed the appeal itself, not merely vacated the stay. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal to Supreme Court 
 
Issues: Whether NCLAT has the jurisdiction to dismiss an appeal under Section 53B merely because the appellant failed to comply 
with a conditional deposit attached to a stay order? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 53B ‚ÄĒ Right to Appeal 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Provides an unconditional statutory right to file an appeal. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No requirement of pre-deposit under the Act as it existed at the time of the case. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 53B(3): Tribunal must decide the appeal on merits, after hearing parties. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Administrative Law Principle 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A statutory appellate right cannot be curtailed by judicial/administrative directions. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Doctrine of Jurisdiction 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Tribunals, being creatures of statute ‚Üí Cannot enlarge their powers beyond what statute authorises. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Law of Conditional Stay 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Non-compliance with a condition attached to a stay order results in ‚Üí Vacation of stay, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NOT ‚Üí Dismissal of appeal. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Pre-deposit was tied only to the stay, not to the appeal 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The stay order was conditional. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The condition governed the stay alone, not the right of appeal. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Failure to deposit ‚Üí stay lapses, nothing more. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 53B gives an unqualified right of appeal 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The right to file an appeal is a statutory right and cannot be made contingent on a monetary deposit unless the 
statute itself requires it. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since the Competition Act did not impose pre-deposit, NCLAT could not. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT exceeded its jurisdiction 
‚ěĘ‚Äč By dismissing the appeal, NCLAT: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Acted beyond the statute. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Added a condition not found in Section 53B. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT, being a creature of statute, must act within the four corners of the statute. 
94 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Mandatory duty under Section 53B(3) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Tribunal is obliged to: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Hear parties. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Decide on merits. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Pass a speaking order. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Dismissal for non-deposit deprives the appellant of this right. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Natural justice 
‚ěĘ‚Äč An opportunity to be heard is built into the statutory appeal. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Dismissing the appeal short-circuits this requirement. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Consequence of non-deposit is limited 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Non-compliance with a stay condition results only in vacation of the stay, not dismissal of the appeal. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, the NCLAT‚Äôs action was patently illegal. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal allowed ‚Üí NCLAT‚Äôs dismissal order set aside. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal restored to NCLAT, with direction to decide on merits. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Stay condition violation ‚Üí only the stay order ceases, but appeal must continue.

‚Äč Whether investigations/inquiries must be conducted within strict time limits.
